content_en:
The second chapter of Riyadh al-Salihin addresses the most indispensable of all spiritual practices: repentance (tawbah). Al-Nawawi's selection of hadiths for this chapter creates a rich portrait of the Islamic theology of repentance — its necessity, its conditions, its rewards, and the extraordinary mercy of Allah toward those who turn to Him.

The commanding Quranic verse that opens the chapter: 'And turn to Allah in repentance, all of you, O believers, that you might succeed' (Quran 24:31) — makes clear that repentance is not a onetime event for the beginning of the spiritual life but an ongoing orientation required of all believers at all times. The Arabic verb used ('tubu' — turn in repentance) is in the present tense, suggesting continuous and repeated turning toward Allah whenever one strays from His path.

The first hadith in this chapter is the most celebrated statement in Islamic tradition on Allah's generosity toward the repentant sinner: 'Allah is more pleased with the repentance of His servant than a man who loses his camel in a barren desert land, then finds it with his food and drink on it and, out of extreme joy, says: O Allah, You are my servant and I am Your Lord — making this mistake due to extreme joy.' (Al-Bukhari and Muslim.) This image of overwhelming divine joy at a servant's return to repentance is one of the most emotionally powerful in the prophetic tradition — Allah does not merely tolerate or accept repentance, He actively rejoices in it.

Al-Nawawi includes the hadith about the man who killed ninety-nine people and then sought repentance — one of the most discussed narrations in Islamic ethics and theology. Despite having killed ninety-nine souls, he was told by a scholar that his repentance was possible, and he was directed to a land of righteous people. He died while literally in the act of migrating there — and because his heart's orientation was sincere repentance and genuine striving, he was forgiven. The angels of mercy were commanded to take his soul, as the distance between his starting point and his destination, when measured, fell on the side of the destination by a handspan. (Al-Bukhari and Muslim.) This hadith establishes that even the greatest sins are within the scope of divine forgiveness when repentance is sincere.

The chapter also includes the hadith establishing the concept of permanent, continual repentance as a Prophetic Sunnah. The Prophet ﷺ, despite being sinless (as Ahl al-Sunnah affirm regarding prophets in the conveyance of revelation), is reported to have said: 'By Allah, I seek Allah's forgiveness and repent to Him more than seventy times a day.' (Al-Bukhari.) If the Prophet ﷺ — the most beloved of Allah's creation — maintained this constant rhythm of seeking forgiveness, the lesson for the believer is unmistakable: istighfar (seeking forgiveness) is not for the sinful alone but is the daily breath of the spiritually alive soul, a continuous acknowledgment of dependence on Allah's mercy and an ongoing renewal of the relationship with the All-Merciful.
